title: Choux pastry, the base recipe
subtitle: The foundational paste for éclairs, profiteroles, gougères
excerpt: The foundational French choux pastry: water, butter and salt brought to the boil; flour stirred in to a tight paste; eggs beaten in to a glossy pipeable dough. Bakes into hollow puffs ready for any sweet or savoury filling. Makes 24 puffs or 12 éclairs.
sourceNotes: Marie-Antoine Carême (1833) and Urbain Dubois, La Cuisine Classique (1856): document the classical French pâte à choux method. Pellaprat, L'Art Culinaire Moderne (pre-1928, public domain): documents the household pipeable form. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845): documents the British adoption.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake the empty shells, cool, freeze in a sealed box; refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes before filling.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 48 puffs or 24 éclairs across two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the shells on the day; the unfilled shells keep crisp in an airtight box for a day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — drops to 170°C for the last 10 minutes

recipeTools.notes: Or beat the eggs in by hand.
A round 12 mm for puffs, a star or round 12 mm for éclairs.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first stage of choux pastry: flour is added to boiling butter-and-water and beaten with a wooden spoon over a low heat until the paste pulls cleanly from the sides of the pan and forms a smooth ball, with a thin film of starch on the base of the pan. This cooks the flour out — gelatinising the starch so it can absorb the eggs that come next. Skip this stage and the dough is gritty; overcook it and the dough dries out.

The cue for when enough egg has been beaten into a choux paste: lift the spoon out of the bowl. The dough should drop in a slow ribbon and the ribbon should hold a 'V' shape briefly before settling. Too stiff (no V) and the eggs are not all in; too soft (no shape at all) and you have gone too far. Add the egg gradually — the last egg goes in by the tablespoon.

content.text: Choux pastry is one of the four foundational French pastries (alongside shortcrust, puff and brioche). The method runs in three stages: bring water, butter and salt to the boil; add the flour and cook it out over a low heat until the paste leaves the pan clean; beat in the eggs one at a time until the paste flows in a slow ribbon. Pipe and bake immediately.
Both the cook-out stage and the egg-test stage matter. Underdone, the dough collapses in the oven; overdone, it bakes solid rather than hollow.

items.prepNote: large, beaten in a jug; use to the egg-test cue (you may not need all four)

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan and line two baking trays. Combine the {{water}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a saucepan. Bring to a rolling boil. Take off the heat. Tip the sifted {{plain-flour}} in all at once and beat hard with a wooden spoon to a smooth paste. Return to a low heat and beat for 2 minutes; the paste should pull cleanly from the sides of the pan and form a smooth ball, with a thin starch film on the base.
Beat in the eggs to the

Tip the paste into a stand-mixer bowl (or a wide mixing bowl). Beat on medium for 2 minutes to cool. Add the beaten {{eggs}} a quarter at a time, beating until each addition is fully incorporated before adding the next. After the third egg, check the texture: when the paste flows in a slow ribbon and holds a brief V on the spoon, you have the right consistency. You may not need the fourth egg; add the last by the tablespoon.

Fill a piping bag fitted with a 12 mm round tip. Pipe 24 walnut-sized puffs (or 12 éclairs, 10 cm long) onto the lined trays, leaving 4 cm between each. Wet a finger and press down any peaks so they do not burn. Bake at 200°C fan for 20 minutes, then drop to 170°C and bake another 10 minutes. The puffs should be deep gold, light when lifted, and feel firm. Pierce each with a skewer to release steam. Cool fully before filling.
